title: Saltfish fritters
subtitle: Jamaican stamp-and-go: crisp salt cod fritters with scotch bonnet
excerpt: Crisp little fritters of flaked salt cod, spring onion, and scotch bonnet bound in a light batter and shallow-fried until golden. Known in Jamaica as stamp-and-go, street food at its most direct.
sourceNotes: Saltfish fritters, known locally as stamp-and-go, are one of the oldest Jamaican street foods, developed from the salt cod (bacalao) introduced to the Caribbean during the colonial trade era. Traditional versions use properly salted and dried cod soaked overnight to remove excess salt. This recipe uses fresh cod fillet with a seasoning adjustment as a substitute, since dried salt cod is rarely stocked outside specialist Caribbean and Portuguese grocers. For the authentic version, use 300 g dried salt cod, soaked overnight in several changes of cold water.

content.text: Stamp-and-go is a name that describes exactly how you eat them: you take one, stamp your foot, and go. These are fast food in the oldest sense, fritters sold from a pan set up at the roadside, eaten immediately, hot from the oil. The batter is simple; the flavour comes from the salt cod, the scotch bonnet, and the thyme.

This version uses fresh cod poached briefly in salted water. It gives a close approximation of salt cod flavour without the overnight soak. For the real thing, use dried salt cod soaked overnight in several changes of cold water. The texture and depth of flavour are better. Either way, the fritters should be very thin, barely thick enough to hold together, so the outside crisps before the inside overcooks.

items.name: Cod fillet
items.prepNote: or 300 g dried salt cod, soaked overnight, see notes

content.text: Place the {{cod-fillet}} of cod in a small saucepan, cover with cold water, add a pinch of salt, and bring to a simmer. Cook for 4 minutes, then drain and leave to cool. When cool enough to handle, flake the flesh into small pieces, discarding any skin and bones.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and {{baking-powder}} of baking powder in a large bowl. Add the {{eggs}} egg, the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{garlic}} of garlic, and {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme. Add enough cold water (roughly 100 to 120 ml) to make a thick batter that just falls from a spoon. Fold in the flaked cod. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide saucepan or deep frying pan over medium heat to around 175°C. Test with a small drop of batter. It should sizzle and rise immediately. Drop tablespoon-sized portions of batter into the oil, 4 to 5 at a time. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side, turning once, until golden brown. Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately.

items.fix: The batter should be thick, almost like a drop scone batter. Add flour a tablespoon at a time until it holds its shape when dropped from a spoon.
items.cause: Batter too thin; not enough egg or flour to bind
items.symptom: Fritters falling apart in the oil

items.fix: The oil must be at 175°C before frying. Too cool and the fritters absorb oil rather than crisping. Keep the batter to dropping consistency, not scooping.
items.cause: Oil not hot enough; batter too thick

items.fix: Season the batter assertively. Add another pinch of sea salt and a splash of hot sauce to the mix. True salt cod will always be more flavourful, worth seeking out from a Caribbean butcher.
items.cause: Fresh cod lacks the salinity of true salt cod

items.fix: Remove the seeds and white pith from the scotch bonnet before chopping. Even deseeded, a scotch bonnet gives significant heat.

content.text: Stamp-and-go is part of the older layer of Jamaican street food, before the jerk stall became the defining image of Jamaican cooking. Salt cod (bacalao) arrived in the Caribbean as cheap, preserved protein in the colonial trade networks that also brought enslaved people. It was adapted into local cooking and became part of the daily diet across the islands. The fritter form is practical: it extends a small amount of fish into a filling snack, and the scotch bonnet, spring onion, and thyme transform the base into something distinctly Jamaican.
